title: Beyond Gates: Pulse Level Quantum Fourier Models
authors: Melvin Strobl, Maja Franz, Lukas Scheller, Eileen Kuehn, Wolfgang Mauerer, Achim Streit
abstract: In the domain of variational quantum algorithms, quantum Fourier models (QFMs) provide a mathematically well defined structure for quantum machine learning (QML). There has been a substantial amount of work on the scalability and trainability of such models showcasing the potential but also the limitations for the prospective application of QFMs. However, much less is known in the context of pulse-level quantum computing, where the microwave parameters that implement unitary operations on the hardware are used to perform computations directly instead of through the interface of quantum circuits. In this work, we evaluate QFMs through the lens of pulse parameters and link metrics such as expressibility and Fourier coefficient correlation (FCC) to this extended set of variational parameters. We show that while control over pulse shapes does not significantly alter the global expressibility or structural correlations of the Ansatz, it fundamentally alters the local optimisation landscape. For composite gates, independent pulse scalings replace a single logical angle by multiple independently tunable sub-angles. This relaxes the rigid monomial couplings induced by the gate-level parameterisation, and provides gradient descent with higher-dimensional escape routes, decoupling local parameter constraints and significantly boosting performance during training. Following an analytical proof, we show numerical results validating our theory on training a QFM with an exponential (ternary) feature map on a Fourier series with the same frequencies.
summary: This paper demonstrates that optimizing microwave pulse parameters instead of standard quantum gates can significantly enhance the training performance of Quantum Fourier Models. By breaking the rigid parameter couplings found in gate-level circuits, pulse-level control provides new directions for gradient descent to escape local minima without altering the model's fundamental expressibility.
main_problem: Investigating how pulse-level control (optimizing microwave parameters) affects the training landscape and trainability of Quantum Fourier Models (QFMs) compared to standard gate-level parameterization.
main_result: Pulse-level control does not significantly change global expressibility but fundamentally improves local trainability by providing higher-dimensional escape routes from local minima, particularly for composite gates.
method: The authors use an analytical framework to link pulse parameters to Fourier coefficients and perform numerical simulations on a 3-qubit system using the Adam optimizer and the Rotating Wave Approximation.
model_or_system: Quantum Fourier Models (QFMs) implemented on superconducting transmon qubits, where the Hamiltonian includes static, drive, and dynamic components, and unitaries are controlled via pulse envelopes.
key_observables: Mean Squared Error (MSE), Fourier Coefficient Correlation (FCC), Expressibility (KL-divergence to 2-design), Jacobian rank, and fidelity/trace distance.
important_parameters: Gate-level angles (theta), pulse parameters (amplitude, width, duration), and pulse-area scaling (lambda).
main_assumptions: The study assumes coherent pulse dynamics, ignores hardware-specific noise/crosstalk, and utilizes the Rotating Wave Approximation (RWA) in a closed-system model.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows improved optimization trajectories and Jacobian rank with pulse control; Figure 2 compares MSE across different ansätze and implementation levels; Figure 3 illustrates frequency activation via pulse variance; Figures 4 and 5 show the sensitivity of fidelity and MSE to pulse parameter distortions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the QFM framework, defines the pulse-level control model, provides an analytical proof regarding escape directions in the optimization landscape, presents numerical simulations comparing gate vs. pulse levels, and concludes with a sensitivity analysis of the metrics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Illustration of our contribution. The optimisation trajectory of a QFM in L can be improved by extending the gate parameters θ through access to pulse parameters Π. This allows to escape local minima and increases the rank of the coefficient Jacobian rank(J). Practically, the combined optimisation of θ ⊕Π consistently lowers the mean squared error (MSE) towards a global optimum for all ansätze examined in this work.
Fig. 3: Count of active frequencies, calculated by the number of high-variance coefficients (see Eq. 3) exceeding a threshold τ > 5 · 10−6. Pulse parameter variance is indicated by color.

Fig. 2: MSE for all ansätze examined in this work, trained either only using unitary gate parameters (without and with a decomposition into basis gates), or in combination with pulse parameters. Results represent the average over ten different seeds used for both the model and data initialisation. Standard deviation is reported via error bars.
Fig. 4: Fidelity between gate- and pulse-level simulations for different levels of distortion σ2 λ, coloured by the number of pulse parameters |Π|.
Fig. 5: MSE over the course of training for different ansätze and using either standard or decomposed unitary gates, or pulse gates. The inset shows a larger view of the first 100 steps.
